messages.subject: [OGRUG] Thinking of starting a useR group in Toronto
messages.subject_clean: [OGRUG] Thinking of starting a useR group in Toronto
messages.body_plain:
Kelvin

In the beginning there were a few of us that knew one another and agreed 
to get together. We advertised on R-help, and asked people to spread the 
word to anyone they knew in the area that might not read r-help. For 
some time there was considerable effort by one individual (Joseph 
Potvin) to get things going. (So I warn you that there may be some 
effort involved.)

There are now some helpful resources available, for example the R SIG 
list for users' groups. John Nash can probably say more about this.

Good luck getting your group going.

Paul Gilbert

Lam, Kelvin wrote:
> Dear folks in Ottawa,
> 
>  
> 
> My name is Kelvin Lam and I have been using R for some time and thinking
> of organizing a useR group in Toronto.  I wonder how you start the group
> and gather users in the Ottawa area in the first place, as most of us in
> the institute or even others mainly deploy SAS.  Any pointers are much
> appreciated.  Thank you very much!!
> 
>  
> 
> Regards,
> 
> Kelvin Lam, MSc.
> 
> Analyst, Programming & Biostatistics
> 
> Institute for Clinical Evaluative Sciences (ICES)
> 
> G179 - 2075 Bayview Avenue
>

>

> 
>

messages.subject: [OGRUG] Starting a RUG
messages.body_plain:
Note that there is now a page on the R-wiki about groups, with an area 
for announcements of people looking to form groups.

http://wiki.r-project.org/rwiki/doku.php?id=rugs:r_user_groups

JN

messages.subject: [OGRUG] extra .
messages.body_plain:
I don't think it does anything.  For readability you might write 6.0 but 
that will not affect the computation.  There are some situations where  
6 is treated as an integer numeric and 6.0 as a double precision 
numeric,  which can be important when you call C or fortran code.    
Underneath I think 6^2 may call different code than 6.0^2 (integer vs 
real arithmetic) but I don't think that applies to sqrt().  In any case, 
you no longer have an integer after the * and  /.

Paul
